title: Monitored quantum transport through a disordered one-dimensional conductor
authors: J. Sánchez Fernán, J. Tworzydło, C. W. J. Beenakker
abstract: We formulate a quantum master equation for the many-particle density matrix of electrons propagating through a single-mode conductor, combining elastic scattering by disorder with time-resolved projective measurements that monitor the outcome of scattering events. The full counting statistics of transmitted electrons has a binomial distribution function, whose mean ${\cal T}$ and variance ${\cal T}(1-{\cal T})$ determine the conductance and shot noise power, respectively. Monitoring suppresses the phase coherence responsible for one-dimensional localization: The decay with conductor length $L$ of the typical transmission probability crosses over at $L\simeq \ell_φ$ from the exponential $e^{-L/ξ}$ (with localization length $ξ$) to the Ohmic $1/L$ decay. Numerical solution of the master equation gives, for weak monitoring, a logarithmic dependence $\ell_φ\simeq ξ\ln(v_{\rm F}τ_φ/ξ)$ of the coherence length $\ell_φ$ on the mean time $τ_φ$ between measurements.
summary: The paper demonstrates that monitoring quantum transport in a 1D disordered wire suppresses Anderson localization, leading to a diffusive regime. It provides a quantitative scaling law for the induced coherence length and shows that the electron transport statistics remain binomial.
main_problem: Investigating how environmental monitoring (time-resolved projective measurements) induces a transition from Anderson localization to classical diffusion in a one-dimensional disordered conductor.
main_result: Monitoring suppresses phase coherence, causing a crossover from exponential localization to Ohmic 1/L decay at a coherence length that scales logarithmically with the measurement interval.
method: The authors develop a quantum master equation approach using Kraus operators and a 1+1 dimensional space-time lattice to track the many-particle density matrix evolution.
model_or_system: A single-mode, one-dimensional electronic conductor with disorder, modeled as a series of scattering segments where decoherence is introduced via periodic projective measurements.
key_observables: Transmission probability (T), conductance (G), shot noise power, and full counting statistics (FCS) of transferred charge.
important_parameters: Conductor length (L), localization length (xi), phase coherence length (l_phi), measurement strength (w), and mean time between measurements (tau_phi).
main_assumptions: The study assumes a low-voltage, linear response regime and a discrete-time evolution model.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a space-time lattice of the conductor; Figure 4 illustrates the crossover from exponential to linear decay in transmission; Figure 5 shows the logarithmic growth of coherence length with measurement strength; Figure 6/7 compare the master equation to the voltage probe model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the master equation framework, applies it to study the transition from localization to diffusion, analyzes the scaling of the coherence length, compares the results to the Büttiker voltage probe model, and discusses reciprocity breaking.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems as it provides a rigorous master equation treatment of how continuous or periodic monitoring affects many-body transport and destroys quantum interference effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Representation on a 1+1 dimensional space-time lat- tice of transport through a single-mode disordered conductor. Scattering centra (extending over a length L) are indicated by filled circles, while open circles represent the ideal leads (no backscattering). Electrons enter the conductor from one end and are either reflected back or transmitted to the other end.

FIG. 2. Scattering phase shift ϕ dependence of the transmis- sion probability T through N = 3 identical tunnel barriers in series, each with tunnel probability Γ = 1 −ρ = 1/2. The effect of decoherence with strength w = √1 −γ is computed from Eq. (6.6).

FIG. 3. Illustration of reciprocity breaking by monitoring [26]. Shown is a three-barrier structure (blue) without left- right symmetry (a thin barrier at the left, two thick barriers at the right). Time-resolved monitoring (on a time scale τc) measures the outcome of the scattering by the thin barrier, broadening the wave packet of the transmitted electron (from eV to ℏ/τc). The broadened wave packet overlaps with the bound state E0 in between the thick barriers. This increases the transmission probability if monitoring happens before the electron reaches the thick barriers (panel a), but not if it happens after (panel b).

FIG. 4. Reciprocal of the typical transmission probability Ttyp = eE[ln T ] as a function of conductor length L. The data points have been computed from Eq. (5.17) for tunnel prob- ability Γ = 0.8 and measurement strength w = 0.03. The expectation value E[ln T ] is an average over 104 random dis- order realizations. The dashed line is a linear fit to the large-L data, to show the crossover to the diffusive regime.

FIG. 5. Coherence length ℓϕ as a function of 1/w2 for three values of the tunnel probability. The solid lines are given by Eq. (7.6), which in dimensionless units reads ℓϕ = ξ ln(1/ξw2). The localization length ξ is not fitted to this data, it is obtained independently from the exponential de- cay of Ttyp ∝e−L/ξ for w = 0.
